claim_verdicts.slide: What AI assistants do well (green zone)
claim_verdicts.bullet: Summarising text is a high-reliability task for current chat tools — LLM summaries consistently match or beat human-written ones
claim_verdicts.issue: 'match or beat' understates source which says 'consistently outperform'
claim_verdicts.suggested_fix: LLM summaries consistently outperform human-written ones

claim_verdicts.slide: What AI assistants do well (green zone)

claim_verdicts.bullet: Chat tools fabricate citations at high rates — even the best models invent 18-29% of references
Give the model three things: what you want to say, who you are writing to, and the tone you want
claim_verdicts.issue: Citation mismatch: source discusses system-message placement and instruction ordering, not an intent/recipient/tone framework. The 'three things' enumeration is not in or derivable from the source.
claim_verdicts.suggested_fix: Drop the <!-- claim:llm_productivity:006 --> citation from this bullet — the advice is sound but unsupported by this source. Alternatively, rewrite the bullet to match the source: 'Set the tone in your prompt and put the task instruction first, with supporting context after.'

claim_verdicts.bullet: Once the model has drafted a reply, review it, check the facts and tone, then edit before you send
claim_verdicts.issue: Source says 'review drafts before sending' but does not enumerate what to check
claim_verdicts.suggested_fix: Once the model has drafted a reply, review it and edit before you send

claim_verdicts.bullet: A bare 'summarise this' gives you a generic recap; specifying what to extract, who will read it, and what format you need improves results by 6-30%
claim_verdicts.issue: Source says 'more than 30%'; applies to structured prompting generally, not summarisation specifically
claim_verdicts.suggested_fix: Structured prompting improves task performance by 6% to over 30% across domains

claim_verdicts.slide: Position bias and the shuffle check
Position bias and the shuffle check
Position bias and the shuffle check

claim_verdicts.bullet: LLMs are unreliable at date and calendar arithmetic: accuracy on basic temporal operations ranges from near-zero to perfect depending on the model and prompting approach
claim_verdicts.issue: Source says 'temporal primitives' and 'prompting conditions', not 'basic temporal operations' and 'prompting approach'
claim_verdicts.suggested_fix: Accuracy on temporal primitives ranges from near-zero to perfect depending on the model and prompting conditions

claim_verdicts.bullet: Tokenizers fragment calendar dates into meaningless pieces — a root cause of errors that can drop accuracy by up to 10 percentage points
claim_verdicts.issue: Source says 'correlates with' not 'root cause'; 10-pp drop applies to 'uncommon dates like historical and futuristic dates' — qualifier dropped
claim_verdicts.suggested_fix: Excessive date fragmentation by tokenizers correlates with accuracy drops of up to 10 percentage points on uncommon dates such as historical and future dates

claim_verdicts.bullet: Even GPT-4 shows a significant gap versus humans on temporal reasoning tasks
claim_verdicts.issue: The 25.2% gap is specific to event temporal reasoning. GPT-4 scores 100% on date arithmetic and near-human on commonsense temporal reasoning. Broad framing contradicts source.
claim_verdicts.suggested_fix: On event temporal reasoning, even GPT-4 trails humans by over 25% — though performance varies across reasoning categories

claim_verdicts.bullet: Samsung banned employee use of ChatGPT after engineers pasted proprietary code, meeting notes, and chip-test data on three separate occasions within 20 days
claim_verdicts.suggested_fix: pasted proprietary code, a meeting transcription, and chip-test sequences
